summary: This encyclopedia entry outlines the life, philosophical development, and core theories of René Descartes. It details his rejection of scholastic Aristotelianism in favor of a mechanistic physics grounded in a new metaphysics, emphasizing mind-body dualism and the use of pure intellect over sensory experience to discover fundamental truths.

principles.statement: Whatever I perceive very clearly and distinctly is true.
principles.rationale: Clear and distinct intellectual perception, independent of the senses, is the ultimate mark of truth and cannot be doubted.
principles.source_quote: I now seem to be able to lay it down as a general rule that whatever I perceive very clearly and distinctly is true

principles.statement: The fundamental truths of reality must be grasped by withdrawing the mind from the senses.
principles.rationale: The senses are designed to detect practical benefits and harms, not to reveal the true essences of substances. Only pure intellect can perceive these essences.
principles.source_quote: withdraw the mind from the senses

principles.statement: To acquire discernment, methodically survey simple things that display order before tackling complex problems.
principles.rationale: By exercising intelligence on simple, orderly products of human skill, the mind learns to combine simple truths to solve complex problems.
principles.source_quote: In order to acquire discernment we should exercise our intelligence by investigating what others have already discovered, and methodically survey even the most insignificant products of human skill, especially those which display order

frameworks.name: Hypothetico-Deductive Method for Natural Philosophy
frameworks.when_to_apply: When explaining particular natural phenomena (like magnetism) where multiple subvisible mechanisms could be responsible.
steps: Derive general principles of physics from innate seeds of truth in the mind.
Use sensory observations to determine the specific properties of the phenomena.
Construct hypotheses about subvisible mechanisms that align with metaphysical constraints.
Test the various hypotheses by their consequences, considering empirical virtues like simplicity and scope.

mental_models.name: The Tree of Philosophy
mental_models.description: A metaphor for the structure and interdependence of human knowledge. Metaphysics forms the roots (foundational principles), physics is the trunk (general laws of nature), and the branches are applied sciences like medicine, mechanics, and morals.
mental_models.example: Descartes used this to argue that the highest moral system presupposes a complete knowledge of all other sciences.

mental_models.name: The Universe as a Machine
mental_models.description: A mechanistic lens viewing the physical universe, including animal and human bodies, as systems composed entirely of matter (extension) in motion, interacting according to universal quantitative laws.
mental_models.example: Explaining physiological processes of human and nonhuman animal bodies through the mechanism of matter arranged into shapes and structures, rather than using 'substantial forms'.

heuristics: Who lives well hidden, lives well.
Rely on the senses to detect immediate benefits and harms to the body, but rely on pure intellect to understand the true nature of reality.

signature_quotes.text: I found myself beset by so many doubts and errors that I came to think I had gained nothing from my attempts to become educated but increasing recognition of my ignorance
signature_quotes.context: Reflecting on his formal education at La Flèche in the Discourse on the Method.
The foundational initial truth discovered in Meditation II, known with certainty because it is clearly and distinctly perceived.

signature_quotes.text: Thus the whole of philosophy is like a tree. The roots are metaphysics, the trunk is physics, and the branches emerging from the trunk are all the other sciences...
signature_quotes.context: From the Author's Letter prefacing the French translation of the Principles of Philosophy.

signature_quotes.text: I now seem to be able to lay it down as a general rule that whatever I perceive very clearly and distinctly is true
signature_quotes.context: Establishing the mark of truth at the beginning of the Third Meditation.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that the senses reveal the essential nature of reality.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: This is a childhood prejudice. The natural function of the senses is merely to inform the mind of what is beneficial or harmful for the mind-body composite, not to reveal the true essences of external objects.

anti_patterns.description: Using 'substantial forms' and 'real qualities' to explain physical phenomena.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: These are imaginary constructs of scholastic Aristotelian philosophy. Physical qualities like heat or color do not really inhere in bodies; they are just effects of matter in motion acting on our nerves.
